pi_items.source: Explicitly requested in the task instructions and verified by the export_result.sh script checks.
Calculated the exact optimal tilt to be 55 degrees using PySAM Pvwattsv8 over the specified grid, which satisfies the expected minimum bound of 50.
Calculated the exact optimal tilt to be 55 degrees using PySAM Pvwattsv8 over the specified grid, which satisfies the expected maximum bound of 65.
Calculated the exact optimal azimuth to be 180 degrees using PySAM Pvwattsv8 over the specified grid, which satisfies the expected minimum bound of 170.
Calculated the exact optimal azimuth to be 180 degrees using PySAM Pvwattsv8 over the specified grid, which satisfies the expected maximum bound of 190.
Calculated maximum winter energy using PySAM Pvwattsv8 over the specified grid is 4207.59 kWh, which satisfies the claimed minimum bound of 2800.
Calculated maximum winter energy using PySAM Pvwattsv8 over the specified grid is exactly 4207.59 kWh, which EXCEEDS the claimed maximum bound of 4200.
Calculated annual energy at the optimal grid point using PySAM Pvwattsv8 is 16442.04 kWh, which satisfies the claimed minimum bound of 14000.
Calculated annual energy at the optimal grid point using PySAM Pvwattsv8 is 16442.04 kWh, which satisfies the claimed maximum bound of 18000.

privileged_info_summary: By running a custom PySAM Pvwattsv8 script on real NREL TMY weather data (phoenix_az_33.450495_-111.983688_psmv3_60_tmy.csv) across the requested grid, I verified the optimal tilt is exactly 55 degrees and the optimal azimuth is exactly 180 degrees. At these optimal parameters, the calculated maximum winter energy (sum of Jan, Feb, Dec) is 4207.59 kWh, and the annual energy is 16442.04 kWh. The task metadata's 'expected_winter_kwh_max' claim of 4200 is explicitly contradicted, as the actual winter energy (4207.59) exceeds this boundary. The weather data used is real historical climate data from the NREL NSRDB.
